text: The surgeon begins a lateral to medial mobilization of the splenic flexure, preparing to safely and efficiently mobilize the colon segment.
The surgeon utilizes three ports, including a twelve millimeter umbilical trocar, for the procedure. A ten millimeter camera compatible with ICG is used for visualization.
The surgeon uses three ports for the procedure, including a left upper quadrant V and a suprapubic XII. The stricture in the proximal descending colon is visible.
The patient is positioned in a reverse Trendelenburg with the left side elevated to utilize gravity during the dissection.
The surgeon carefully dissects the lateral attachments of the colon, progressing upwards during the splenic flexure mobilization.
The surgeon switches to a super-musicalic approach after reaching the limit of visibility from the previous direction, carefully preserving the omentum while continuing the dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the gastrocolic ligament, distal to the epiploic arcade, while preserving the omentum, making the procedure more tedious but maintaining the integrity of the omental tissue.
The surgeon proceeds to dissect and remove the remaining lineal colic and renal colic ligaments, using the established port placements in the left upper quadrant and suprapubic region.
The surgeon selects the proximal and distal margins for resection, with a visible small hole in the mesentery.
The surgeon observes the prominent blood supply to the area and widens the hole in the mesentery, located just distal to the left branch of the middle colic artery.
The surgeon selects the distal and proximal margins of the colon, ensuring precise excision of the mesentery to avoid excessive removal that could lead to ischemia.

The surgeon carefully preserves the ascending branch of the left colic artery visible on the right below the ligatures, while continuing to dissect and remove the remaining mesentery up to the proximal margin.
The surgeon observes palpable or pulsatile blood flow to the left of the ligature, indicating a reassuring blood supply in the area.
The surgeon positions the stapler in place in preparation for checking indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence, ensuring the stapler is ready for use.
The surgeon observes the chosen margin with good blood supply, noting that the proximal bowel appears slightly more dilated compared to the distal bowel.
The surgeon uses two firings of the stapler to transect the dilated proximal bowel, following a period of clear liquids and full bowel preparation.
The surgeon positions the stapler to staple just distal to the stricture, where the bowel caliber visibly changes and appears much less dilated.
The surgeon aligns the isoparastaltic anastomosis in preparation for the next step of the procedure.
The surgeon places stay sutures and makes a collotomy incision on the descending colon, followed by a similar incision on the transverse colon.
The surgeon inserts a stapler with a gold vascular load to staple the tissue distal to the stricture, observing the change in caliber as the area becomes less dilated.
The surgeon performs the common channel anastomosis using a vascular load stapler, with all stapling conducted through the suprapubic port.
The surgeon prepares to upsize the suprapubic port for specimen extraction, while creating a wide anastomosis between the descending and transverse colon.

The surgeon inspects the surgical site for hemostasis, ensuring there is no active bleeding. The camera is positioned at the suprapubic location to provide optimal angles for suturing the incision closed.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 suture to secure the stay suture, which is pulled up through the anterior abdominal wall to stabilize the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to sew around the near corner of the bowel, using a transition stitch to close the anterior wall with an over-and-over technique.
The surgeon carefully closes the lip of the anastomosis, then proceeds to imbricate the tissue, ensuring a secure closure.

The surgeon switches the camera to the umbilical position and sutures from the left upper quadrant and suprapubic port, running the suture line down to ensure complete coverage of the initial closure area, focusing on the most tenuous portion of the anastomosis.
The surgeon checks the fluorescence of the tissue using indocyanine green (ICG) imaging to assess perfusion and ensure adequate blood supply to the anastomosis site.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis, ensuring it lays without tension, and covers it with preserved omentum. The specimen is then prepared for suprapubic extraction.
